verdict.summary: A good business at a fair price — not a great business at a great price. Starter position only at today's quote; back up the truck on AV-fear sell-offs.

pros: Asset-light marketplace converting **$10B+ FCF** on $54B revenue (18.8% FCF margin).[^uber-10k-2024]
Network-effect moat: 2–3× Lyft share in US, #1/2 in most of 70+ countries.[^uber-investor-day-2024]
Embedded high-margin **advertising business** (~$1.5B run-rate) compounding fastest.[^uber-q4-2024-pr]
Capital return inflection: **$6.5B buyback in 2025** after first-ever in 2024.[^uber-q4-2024-pr]
ROIC 19% vs WACC 10% — real economic profit.
cons: **Autonomous vehicles** — Tesla/Waymo could bypass the demand layer. Genuine unknown.[^waymo-milestone-2025]
**Driver classification** regulation risk (PRO Act, EU PWD enforcement).[^ab5-prop22]
SBC of **$1.86B/yr** dilutes real shareholder yield from 5% to ~3%.[^uber-10k-2024]
Insiders own only **0.17%**; no founder remains on the cap table.[^uber-def14a-2025]
Insurance accruals are actuarial and lumpy — CA verdicts can swing margin 200bp.[^uber-10k-2024]

industry.title: Industry primer — global on-demand mobility & delivery
industry.intro: Uber sits across three industries that share one substrate (a marketplace matching independent operators with consumer demand) but have very different economics, competitors, and growth curves. Understand the shape of each before scoring the stock.

sizeAndShape.title: 1. How big are these markets today?
sizeAndShape.body: **Ride-hail** is a ~$140B global GMV market[^wiki-ridesharing] dominated by ~6 regional champions; **online food delivery** is a larger but lower-margin ~$200B market with more fragmented leadership[^wiki-online-food]. Both are mature in developed markets (high-single-digit growth), still scaling in emerging markets. **Freight brokerage** is a separate, much more cyclical ~$110B North-American market that Uber entered via Uber Freight (2017). **Autonomous vehicles** is the wild card — small today (~250k rides/week at Waymo) but doubling year-on-year and the central reason Uber's multiple is compressed.

segmentation.title: 2. How the industry segments
segmentation.intro: Four orthogonal axes — confusion on any one causes mispricing of mobility platforms.

segments.title: Mobility · Delivery · Freight · Adjacent (AV, Ads, Grocery)
segments.body:
**Mobility (~57% of Uber rev, $83B GMV)** — ride-hail, taxi-dispatch, scheduled/Reserve, premium tiers, scooters/bikes. Highest take-rate (~28-30%), best-margin business. Network-effects strongest here.

**Delivery (~37% of Uber rev, $74B GMV)** — restaurant + grocery + retail courier. Lower take-rate (~18-20%), thinner margins, more competitive. Regional leadership matters more than global.

**Freight (~6% of Uber rev)** — digital brokerage, B2B not B2C. Margin-thin (~10-12% take), commoditised, deeply cyclical. Uber Freight is sub-scale vs C.H. Robinson, J.B. Hunt, RXO.

**Adjacent layers** — high-margin **advertising** (~$1.5B run-rate, growing 60%+), **Uber One** subscription, **Uber for Business**, **Uber Health**, **autonomous-vehicle network orchestration** (Waymo PHX partnership). These are the mix-shift story.
segments.implications: **Mobility is the cash engine.** Delivery is a growth + defense moat (cross-sell, frequency). Freight is sub-scale and may not stay strategic. Advertising is the **non-obvious tailwind** — high-margin, lightly cyclical, and the only meaningful new earnings driver inside the existing footprint.

segments.title: Where Uber operates directly vs holds equity in a regional champion
segments.body:
**Direct operations (~70+ countries)** — US, Canada, Mexico, most of LatAm, UK, EU, Australia, NZ, Japan, India, MENA (via Careem 2019 acquisition), most of Africa.[^wiki-uber]

**Equity stakes in regional rivals (where Uber sold and exited)** — **DiDi (~12% stake)** for China (sold 2016 for ~$7B GAAP value), **Grab (~14%)** for SEA (sold 2018), **Yandex Taxi** Russia/CIS (merged 2018, divested 2022).[^wiki-didi][^wiki-grab]

**Pure competition (Uber operates and a credible local rival also exists)** — Lyft (US, ~25% share), Bolt (EU/Africa), Ola (India), 99 (Brazil, owned by DiDi), Free Now (EU, Daimler/BMW JV).
segments.implications: **Uber is a US-anchored global operator with passive exposure to China + SEA via equity stakes.** The pure-competition markets (US, EU, India) are where price wars happen and margins flex. Equity-stake markets contribute **non-cash mark-to-market**, not operating cash flow.

segments.title: Marketplace independent contractor vs employed driver
segments.body:
**Independent contractor / marketplace model (~95%+ of global driver supply)** — drivers set their own hours, use their own vehicles, are paid per trip. Lighter cost, no benefits, no unemployment insurance. This is Uber's default model and the **single biggest cost-structure advantage**.

**Employed / EOR model** — required in select markets after legal challenges: France (Cour de Cassation 2020 ruling on a specific case), Spain (2021 'Rider Law'), UK (Supreme Court 2021 — workers, not employees, intermediate status). Operating in these markets is materially more expensive — ~25-35% higher labour cost per trip.[^ab5-prop22]

**Hybrid / regulated** — California's Prop 22 (2020, upheld 2024) carved out a third category for app-based drivers — independent + minimum wage floor + healthcare stipend. **The political contest is whether Prop 22 spreads or AB5 spreads.**
segments.implications: **A Federal PRO Act-style reclassification is the single largest non-AV downside.** It would compress US Mobility margin by 600-1,000 bps and is impossible to fully model — but is currently low-probability under the present US administration.

segments.title: AV provider vs demand aggregator vs consumer brand — the central debate
segments.body:
Today, Uber is the **consumer brand + demand aggregator + driver marketplace** — a vertically integrated platform between rider and supply. Three plausible futures:

1. **Status quo (Uber wins)** — AV providers (Waymo, Aurora, etc.) have **no consumer brand and no scaled demand**. They sell capacity wholesale to Uber, which keeps the consumer relationship. Uber's PHX deal with Waymo (2024) is the proof point.

2. **Disintermediation (Uber loses demand layer)** — Tesla, with 100M+ installed FSD-eligible vehicles, launches a Tesla Network bypassing Uber. Or Waymo scales direct-to-consumer in major metros. Uber retains long-tail markets but loses dense urban demand.[^waymo-milestone-2025]

3. **Hybrid (most likely)** — Uber loses ~30-40% of dense urban share over 5-10 years to direct-to-consumer AV apps; retains long-tail + suburban + multi-modal + delivery. EBITDA flat in absolute terms but multiple compresses on growth-rate fears.
segments.implications: **This axis IS the Uber thesis.** Bull case: Uber's demand-aggregation scale is the moat (CAC for AV providers to reach consumers is high). Bear case: Tesla bypasses entirely. **Genuine unknown** — size position accordingly.

competitive.title: 3. Who else is in the market?
competitive.intro: **Uber is the global leader in mobility GMV** (when you exclude China and SEA, where it sold and holds equity). In delivery it's #1 outside the US and #2 inside. In freight it's a sub-scale entrant. AVs is a parallel race entirely.

leader.name: Uber Technologies (mobility + delivery + freight)
leader.body: **$162B GMV FY24 across all segments.** ~75% US ridehail share[^wiki-ridesharing], #1 ride-hail in 70+ countries (UK, FR, DE, AU, NZ, JP, MX, BR, AR, ZA, MENA via Careem). #2 US food delivery (~25% share). Holds equity stakes in DiDi (~12%) and Grab (~14%) covering China + SEA — non-operating mark-to-market.

followers.name: Lyft (US ride-hail)
followers.body: **Single-country US-only operator.** Smaller scale → higher driver supply cost per trip → structurally worse take-rate. Regularly tries price wars; loses each cycle. Surfaced bid speculation periodically (DoorDash, others). Not a viable disruptor at scale.[^wiki-lyft]

followers.name: DoorDash (US delivery #1)
followers.body: **The clear US delivery winner.** Restaurant supply density + courier density advantages compound; Uber Eats has structurally lost US share since 2020 and is a clear #2 (~25%). DoorDash has been expanding internationally (Wolt acquisition 2022 in EMEA) — a long-term concern in Uber Delivery's #1-ex-US fortress.[^wiki-doordash]

followers.name: DiDi Global (China)
followers.body: **Owns China outright** after Uber sold its China ops in 2016 for an equity stake. Also dominates Brazil via 99. Regulatory shadow from 2021 China cybersecurity probe limits expansion outside core. Uber's stake is mark-to-market in 'Other income'.[^wiki-didi]

followers.name: Grab (SEA), Bolt (EU/Africa), Ola (India)
followers.body: **Grab** — SEA's super-app, owns SEA ride-hail + delivery. Uber sold to them 2018, holds ~14%.[^wiki-grab] **Bolt** — Estonian rival, EU + Africa, hyper-aggressive on take-rate. **Ola** — India rival, currently weaker than Uber India after 2023 layoffs.

followers.name: Just Eat Takeaway, Delivery Hero, Meituan, Zomato/Swiggy (intl delivery)
followers.type: Various — listed in EU, DE, China, India
followers.body: **Just Eat Takeaway** (UK/EU; sold Grubhub 2024 to Wonder), **Delivery Hero** (Foodpanda + global brands; Taiwan deal blocked 2024[^foodpanda-taiwan-2024]), **Meituan** (China dominant), **Zomato + Swiggy** (India duopoly). Uber Eats competes head-to-head with these region-by-region; wins in MEA and parts of LATAM, loses in CN and IN.

followers.name: Waymo (Alphabet) — AV ride-hail
followers.body: **~250k paid rides/week mid-2024**, doubling year-on-year. Operating in PHX, SF, LA. Uber **partners** with Waymo in Phoenix (Uber app dispatches Waymo cars). The partnership is the bull case — Waymo provides supply, Uber provides demand. The bear case: Waymo eventually bypasses Uber in dense urban markets.[^waymo-milestone-2025]

followers.name: Tesla, Cruise (paused), Pony.ai, Aurora, Mobileye — other AV
followers.body: **Tesla** — FSD beta + announced 'Cybercab'; the largest existential bear case for Uber if it launches a Tesla Network at scale. **Cruise (GM)** — paused after Oct 2023 SF incident. **Aurora** — partner with Uber on freight AVs.[^aurora-atg-2020] **Pony.ai, WeRide** — China AV leaders. **Mobileye** — chips + sensor stack supplier.

growth.title: 4. How is the industry growing?
growth.body:
**Ride-hail is in a healthy mid-cycle.** Vehicle-ownership decline among Gen Z + urban density + tourism rebound + hybrid-work commute patterns drive **8-10% volume growth** in developed markets, **15-20%** in emerging.[^wiki-ridesharing] Take rates have stabilised after the 2018-21 driver-incentive wars.

**Food delivery is normalising.** Post-COVID growth fell from +25% (2020-21) to **+5-7%** as restaurants reopened. Industry is consolidating (Just Eat sold Grubhub; Wolt acquired by DoorDash; Foodpanda-Taiwan blocked) — fewer subsidies, better unit economics for survivors.[^wiki-online-food]

**Freight is cyclical-down.** US freight rates collapsed 2022-24, cleared the weak hands (Convoy closed Oct 2023). 2025-26 should see a recovery cycle.

**AVs is the generational unknown.** Best-case scenario: AVs **expand the mobility TAM 3-5×** by lowering per-mile cost and unlocking new use cases (kids, elderly, no-license riders). Worst-case: AVs **disintermediate** the demand aggregator. The truth is probably in between — but the market is currently pricing the bear case at a 30-40% probability, which is what compresses Uber's multiple.[^waymo-milestone-2025]

drivers.label: Vehicle-ownership decline
drivers.body: Gen Z licence-holding lowest in 30 yrs; urban density rising

drivers.label: Mix-shift to ads + subscription
drivers.body: Uber Ads $1.5B run-rate, Uber One ~25M subs — high-margin extensions

drivers.label: Emerging-market expansion
drivers.body: India, MENA (Careem), LATAM still in single-digit penetration

drivers.label: Worker-classification regulation
drivers.body: EU PWD enforcement variable; PRO Act dormant under current admin

drivers.label: Insurance cost cycle
drivers.body: CA verdicts can swing margin 200bps; long-tail accruals lumpy

drivers.label: Tesla / Waymo direct-to-consumer
drivers.body: Existential question; current data inconclusive but the central debate

implications.title: What this means for Uber
implications.body:
Uber is **the global leader in mobility, the #1-ex-US leader in delivery, and a sub-scale entrant in freight** — but its multiple isn't pricing leadership; it's pricing the **AV disintermediation tail risk**.

Practical implication for sizing: this is a **'good business at a fair price'** rather than a great business at a great price. Position 2-3% at today's quote. **Scale to 5-6% on AV-fear sell-offs** that drop the price below $65. The asymmetry is real but conditional — own it for the cash flow + buyback yield, hedge the AV outcome by buying lower not bigger.

sections.label: Section 1 of 3
sections.title: Business · Do I truly understand it?

questions.title: What does Uber actually do?
questions.body: Uber runs an app that matches three groups who have something to give with three groups who need it: **riders ↔ drivers**, **eaters ↔ restaurants ↔ couriers**, **shippers ↔ truckers**. It owns the matching engine — maps, pricing, ratings, fraud checks — and skims ~25–30¢ of every dollar that flows through.[^uber-10k-2024]

questions.title: How do they make money, and from whom?
questions.sub: Who pays, for what, at what margin.
questions.footnote: Hidden engine: **advertising** (~$1.5B run-rate, very high incremental margin) — most under-appreciated lever in the P&L.[^uber-q4-2024-pr]

questions.title: Am I pretending to understand it, or do I really?

left: Why Mobility > Delivery as a business (denser network, less SKUs)
Why asset-light platforms compound FCF post-scale
right: Gross-vs-net revenue accounting by jurisdiction (real complexity)
True unit economics in an AV world (driver line gone, but exclusivity over supply gone too)

questions.title: Happy owning it 10 years if the market shut tomorrow?
questions.body: Yes — provided management doesn't burn $10B trying to "own AVs too," driver-classification rules don't get globally codified into employment, and buybacks continue meaningfully shrinking share count. The 10-year window is exactly when the AV question resolves; Uber is positioned on _both_ sides as partner and demand aggregator.

questions.title: What could kill this business?
riskMatrix: Consumer-app switching cost; Uber's demand-pattern data
Delivery now profitable, not a cash sink

sections.label: Section 2 of 3
sections.title: Management · Can I trust these people with my capital?

questions.title: Trust them to run my family's money?
questions.body: **Dara Khosrowshahi** (CEO since Aug 2017) inherited a dumpster fire from Travis Kalanick. Eight years later: GAAP profitable, S&P 500 (Dec 2023)[^sp500-add-2023], $10B FCF, no major scandals. From Expedia, where he was a respected low-drama operator (2005–2017).[^khosrowshahi-bio-2017] New CFO **Prashanth Mahendra-Rajah** (2024) comes from Analog Devices — manufacturing-discipline DNA, fitting Uber's evolution from growth-at-all-costs to compound-cash.

left: Killed dual-class super-vote in 2023 — one share, one vote[^uber-def14a-2025]
Segment-level economics disclosed in plain English since 2022
right: Foodpanda Taiwan deal blocked by regulators Dec 2024 — still an M&A itch[^foodpanda-taiwan-2024]

timeline.note: Burned ~$10B on China, SE Asia, ATG. Disaster, but instructive.
Sold China→Didi, SE Asia→Grab, Russia→Yandex, ATG→Aurora[^aurora-atg-2020] — for equity. Bought Postmates ($2.65B), Cornershop. **Excellent.**
May-22 "sea change" memo: cost discipline, meritocracy at promotion.[^dara-sea-change-2022] Stopped speculative M&A. **Strong.**
Initiated buybacks, paid down converts, Foodpanda Taiwan blocked.[^foodpanda-taiwan-2024] **Good so far, watch closely.**

questions.title: Do they tell the truth in good years and bad?
bullets: **FY22 (bad):** $9.1B GAAP loss from equity write-downs — Dara owned it, didn't hide behind non-GAAP.[^uber-10k-2022]
**FY23 (breakthrough):** Did _not_ spike the football; warned 2023 GAAP NI was flattered by equity revaluations and wouldn't repeat.[^uber-10k-2023]
**FY24:** Led with the disclosure that $5.8B of NI came from a deferred-tax valuation allowance release — a one-time non-cash gain. Could have buried it. Didn't.[^uber-10k-2024][^uber-letter-2024]

questions.title: Real skin in the game?
questions.body: Total insider ownership **0.17%** (~$258M).[^uber-def14a-2025] Dara holds ~1.5M shares + options (~$110–150M) — meaningful for him but he sells programmatically every quarter via 10b5-1. **Travis Kalanick exited his entire 8.6% stake by 2020.**[^kalanick-exit-2019] No founder remains. Compare Meta (Zuckerberg 13%), Amazon (Bezos 9%). This is firmly the "professionally-managed" camp — incentives run through comp plan design, not equity ownership. The 2026 proxy is worth reading: TSR-linked PSUs = positive signal; pure RSU grants = neutral-negative.

sections.label: Section 3 of 3
sections.title: Price · Am I paying a sensible price?

questions.title: What am I paying today?
questions.sub: P/E, earnings yield, or whichever lens fits.
questions.body: Most honest single number: **16× EV/FCF** on a business that compounded FCF ~25% over four years. Not expensive. Not the bargain it was at $40 in 2022. **Fair.**

questions.title: What is the market implicitly expecting?
questions.body: Reverse-DCF: today's price requires ~10–12% FCF CAGR for 10 years, then 3% terminal. My base case: 15–18% near-term, 10–12% mid-term FCF growth as Delivery scales and advertising mixes up. Conclusion: **the market is pricing roughly what I'd model — no big upside or downside embedded.** The 52-week -18.6% reflects fears about Tesla robotaxi (Austin) and Waymo (SF/LA), in my view over-discounted: Waymo today ~250k rides/wk[^waymo-milestone-2025] vs Uber ~33M/day globally.[^uber-q4-2024-pr]

questions.title: What would I pay for the whole business?
questions.footnote: Today's quote ≈ what a rational private buyer would pay. Nibble below **$65** (13% MOS, ~13× EV/FCF). Back up the truck below **$55** (~11× EV/FCF on a 15%-grower).

questions.title: At what price would I sell, and why?

triggers.title: Fundamental trigger
triggers.body: FCF margin contracts 2 consecutive Qs with no one-off cause → AV take-rate compression is real → exit.

triggers.title: Capital allocation trigger
triggers.body: > $10B acquisition or re-entry into China / own AV fleet → exit.

questions.title: If it drops 40% next month — buy or panic?
questions.body: $45 ≈ market cap $92B, EV/FCF ~9×. **Pre-commitment: buy aggressively,** unless the drop is accompanied by one of:
ordered: Federal US or top-3 market driver-reclassification ruling
Step-function AV consumer adoption (Tesla 100k+ public robotaxis or Waymo signing a Tesla-style OEM deal + direct apps in 10+ cities)
Dara departure / large insider sale / surprise CFO change
questions.footnote: The pre-commitment matters more than the analysis. Most investors fail not by picking wrong, but by selling at $45 and buying back at $90.

scorecard.summary: Position sizing: 2–3% at today's quote; scale to 5–6% on AV-fear sell-off. Conviction: Medium-High on business, Medium on management, Medium on price.

sources.title: Uber Technologies, Inc. — Annual Report (Form 10-K) for FY2024
sources.note: Filed with SEC EDGAR (CIK 1543151); access via Uber IR SEC filings index. Source for FCF, SBC, segment economics, insurance reserve commentary, and risk factors.

sources.title: Uber Technologies, Inc. — Annual Report (Form 10-K) for FY2023
sources.note: Filed with SEC EDGAR (CIK 1543151). FY23 was the breakthrough profitability year; management called out non-repeatable equity revaluations.

sources.title: Uber Technologies, Inc. — Annual Report (Form 10-K) for FY2022
sources.note: Filed with SEC EDGAR (CIK 1543151). $9.1B GAAP loss; equity-investment write-downs disclosed transparently.

sources.title: Uber Announces Results for Q4 and Full Year 2024 — press release
sources.note: Source for $6.5B buyback announcement, ~$1.5B advertising run-rate, and daily-trip cadence. Earnings-call transcript linked from this page.

sources.title: Dara Khosrowshahi — 2024 Letter to Shareholders (in 2025 Proxy front matter)
sources.note: Shareholder letter is in the front matter of the 2025 Proxy (DEF 14A); browse Uber IR SEC filings index and open the 2025 Proxy. Tone-and-honesty read: led with deferred-tax allowance disclosure rather than burying it.

sources.title: Uber Technologies — 2025 Proxy Statement (DEF 14A)
sources.note: Filed with SEC EDGAR (CIK 1543151) as DEF 14A. Source for insider ownership %, executive comp design (TSR PSUs vs RSUs), board composition.

sources.title: Uber 2024 Investor Day — Long-term financial framework
sources.note: Long-term outlook for FCF conversion, Mobility/Delivery EBITDA targets, geographic share data.

sources.title: Uber — "hard pivot" memo: 2022 cost-discipline shift (company history)
sources.note: The May 2022 internal "sea-change" memo from Dara Khosrowshahi (shift from growth-at-all-costs to FCF discipline) is documented in the History section with primary-source citations. Memo was widely reported across tech press.

sources.title: Aurora Innovation acquires Uber's ATG self-driving unit; Uber takes Aurora equity stake (Dec 2020)
sources.note: Capital-allocation milestone: Uber exited the most expensive ATG investment cycle for equity rather than cash. See "Uber ATG acquisition" section with primary-source citations.

sources.title: Taiwan FTC blocks Uber's $950M acquisition of Foodpanda Taiwan (Dec 2024)
sources.note: Regulatory block signal — capital-allocation 'M&A itch' yellow flag for the Dara P3 era. See Foodpanda "Taiwan operations" section with primary-source citations from Reuters and Taiwan FTC.

sources.title: Waymo One — paid rides per week across PHX, SF, LA (2025 cadence)
sources.note: Useful for the AV-pacing argument: Waymo at ~250k/week vs Uber's ~33M/day globally — orders-of-magnitude gap. Cross-reference: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waymo

sources.title: Uber added to S&P 500 (December 2023)
sources.note: S&P 500 inclusion confirmed Uber as a benchmark constituent — passive flows + investor-universe expansion. See "Selection criteria" and historical additions; cross-reference: List of S&P 500 companies.

sources.title: Dara Khosrowshahi — Expedia CEO appointed to lead Uber (Aug 2017)
sources.note: Background on Dara's Expedia tenure (2005–2017) and the original Uber turnaround mandate. Wikipedia article cites primary sources (NYT, Bloomberg, Expedia filings).

sources.title: Travis Kalanick — full Uber stake sale and board departure (Dec 2019)
sources.note: Founder exit confirmation — establishes the 'no founder remains' status for Q2.5. Wikipedia article cites primary Reuters/Bloomberg coverage of the post-lockup share sales and board exit.

sources.title: California Assembly Bill 5 (AB5) and Proposition 22 — gig-worker classification background
sources.note: Statutory and ballot-initiative basis for the U.S. driver-reclassification risk; Prop 22 upheld by CA Supreme Court (2024).

sources.title: Uber — Wikipedia
sources.note: Country-by-country operating footprint, M&A history (Careem MENA 2019, Postmates 2020, Drizly 2021, Foodpanda Taiwan blocked 2024), equity stakes (DiDi China 2016, Grab SEA 2018, Yandex Taxi divested 2022).

sources.title: Ridesharing company — Wikipedia
sources.note: Industry overview — global ride-hail market sizing (~$140B GMV), regional leadership map (Uber, DiDi, Grab, Bolt, Ola, Yandex), historical waves of consolidation.

sources.title: Online food ordering — Wikipedia
sources.note: Industry overview — global online food-delivery market (~$200B GMV), post-COVID growth normalisation, regional leaders (DoorDash, Uber Eats, Just Eat Takeaway, Delivery Hero, Meituan, Zomato/Swiggy).

sources.title: DoorDash — Wikipedia
sources.note: US food-delivery #1 (~65% share). Wolt acquisition 2022 expanded EMEA footprint. Direct competitor to Uber Eats in US; emerging competitor outside US.

sources.title: Lyft — Wikipedia
sources.note: US-only ride-hail #2 (~22-25% share). Single-market scale disadvantage vs Uber's global supply density.

sources.title: DiDi Global — Wikipedia
sources.note: China ride-hail dominant (~90% share). Acquired Uber's China ops 2016 (Uber holds ~12% equity stake). Owns 99 in Brazil. Delisted from NYSE 2022 after China cybersecurity probe.

sources.title: Grab — Wikipedia
sources.note: SEA super-app (ride-hail + delivery + payments). Acquired Uber's SEA ops 2018 (Uber holds ~14% equity stake). Listed NASDAQ 2021 via SPAC.
